execution_details.stdout:
#	$OpenBSD: sshd_config,v 1.104 2021/07/02 05:11:21 dtucker Exp $

# This is the sshd server system-wide configuration file.  See
# sshd_config(5) for more information.

# This sshd was compiled with PATH=/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/usr/local/sbin:/usr/sbin

# The strategy used for options in the default sshd_config shipped with
# OpenSSH is to specify options with their default value where
# possible, but leave them commented.  Uncommented options override the
# default value.

# To modify the system-wide sshd configuration, create a  *.conf  file under
#  /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/  which will be automatically included below
Include /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf

# If you want to change the port on a SELinux system, you have to tell
# SELinux about this change.
# semanage port -a -t ssh_port_t -p tcp #PORTNUMBER
#
#Port 22
#AddressFamily any
#ListenAddress 0.0.0.0
#ListenAddress ::

#HostKey /etc/ssh/ssh_host_rsa_key
#HostKey /etc/ssh/ssh_host_ecdsa_key
#HostKey /etc/ssh/ssh_host_ed25519_key

# Ciphers and keying
#RekeyLimit default none

# Logging
#SyslogFacility AUTH
#LogLevel INFO

# Authentication:

#LoginGraceTime 2m
#PermitRootLogin prohibit-password
#StrictModes yes
#MaxAuthTries 6
#MaxSessions 10

#PubkeyAuthentication yes

# The default is to check both .ssh/authorized_keys and .ssh/authorized_keys2
# but this is overridden so installations will only check .ssh/authorized_keys
AuthorizedKeysFile	.ssh/authorized_keys

#AuthorizedPrincipalsFile none

#AuthorizedKeysCommand none
#AuthorizedKeysCommandUser nobody

# For this to work you will also need host keys in /etc/ssh/ssh_known_hosts
#HostbasedAuthentication no
# Change to yes if you don't trust ~/.ssh/known_hosts for
# HostbasedAuthentication
#IgnoreUserKnownHosts no
# Don't read the user's ~/.rhosts and ~/.shosts files
IgnoreRhosts yes

# To disable tunneled clear text passwords, change to no here!
#PasswordAuthentication yes
#PermitEmptyPasswords no

# Change to no to disable s/key passwords
#KbdInteractiveAuthentication yes

# Kerberos options
#KerberosAuthentication no
#KerberosOrLocalPasswd yes
#KerberosTicketCleanup yes
#KerberosGetAFSToken no
#KerberosUseKuserok yes

# GSSAPI options
#GSSAPIAuthentication no
#GSSAPICleanupCredentials yes
#GSSAPIStrictAcceptorCheck yes
#GSSAPIKeyExchange no
#GSSAPIEnablek5users no

# Set this to 'yes' to enable PAM authentication, account processing,
# and session processing. If this is enabled, PAM authentication will
# be allowed through the KbdInteractiveAuthentication and
# PasswordAuthentication.  Depending on your PAM configuration,
# PAM authentication via KbdInteractiveAuthentication may bypass
# the setting of "PermitRootLogin without-password".
# If you just want the PAM account and session checks to run without
# PAM authentication, then enable this but set PasswordAuthentication
# and KbdInteractiveAuthentication to 'no'.
# WARNING: 'UsePAM no' is not supported in RHEL and may cause several
# problems.
#UsePAM no

#AllowAgentForwarding yes
#AllowTcpForwarding yes
#GatewayPorts no
#X11Forwarding no
#X11DisplayOffset 10
#X11UseLocalhost yes
#PermitTTY yes
#PrintMotd yes
#PrintLastLog yes
#TCPKeepAlive yes
#PermitUserEnvironment no
#Compression delayed
#ClientAliveInterval 0
#ClientAliveCountMax 3
#UseDNS no
#PidFile /var/run/sshd.pid
#MaxStartups 10:30:100
#PermitTunnel no
#ChrootDirectory none
#VersionAddendum none

# no default banner path
#Banner none

# override default of no subsystems
Subsystem	sftp	/usr/libexec/openssh/sftp-server

# Example of overriding settings on a per-user basis
#Match User anoncvs
#	X11Forwarding no
#	AllowTcpForwarding no
#	PermitTTY no
#	ForceCommand cvs server

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
We are at attempt 3 (index 2 in 0-indexed list of attempts). We must use a different approach from the previous two attempts.

Previous attempts:
  Attempt 1: 
      Commands: ['f /etc/ssh/sshd_config ]; then echo "LogLevel VERBOSE" > /etc/ssh/sshd_config; else sed -i "s/^s*#?s*LogLevels*.*$/LogLevel VERBOSE/" /etc/ssh/sshd_config; if ! grep -q "^s*#?s*LogLevels*" /etc/ssh/sshd_config; then echo "LogLevel VERBOSE', "etc/ssh/sshd_config; fi; fi'"
  Attempt 2:
      Commands: 
          - sed -i '/^\s*LogLevel\s\+/Id' /etc/ssh/sshd_config
          - sed -i '/^\s*LogLevel\s\+/Id' /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf
          - echo "LogLevel VERBOSE" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf
          - grep -i ^LogLevel /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf

We must avoid repeating any of these previously-tried commands.

Therefore, for attempt 3, we cannot use:
  - The command from attempt 1: ['f /etc/ssh/sshd_config ]; then echo "LogLevel VERBOSE" > /etc/ssh/sshd_config; else sed -i "s/^s*#?s*LogLevels*.*$/LogLevel VERBOSE/" /etc/ssh/sshd_config; if ! grep -q "^s*#?s*LogLevels*" /etc/ssh/sshd_config; then echo "LogLevel VERBOSE', "etc/ssh/sshd_config; fi; fi'"
  - The commands from attempt 2: 
        sed -i '/^\s*LogLevel\s\+/Id' /etc/ssh/sshd_config
        sed -i '/^\s*LogLevel\s\+/Id' /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf
        echo "LogLevel VERBOSE" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf
        grep -i ^LogLevel /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf

So we must design a new set of commands.

We are to set the SSH Daemon LogLevel to VERBOSE.

We have the following functions available: run_cmd, read_file, write_file.

We must use run_cmd for exactly one shell command at a time.

We must use read_file to inspect the target config file before modifying it.

We are to write the fix to a file.

Plan:
  We will:
      1. Use read_file to read the current /etc/ssh/sshd_config file.
      2. Check if the file contains a line that sets LogLevel (exactly: starts with optional whitespace, then "LogLevel", then optional whitespace, then an equals sign, then optional whitespace, then a value, then optional whitespace, then end of line).
      3. If it does, we will use sed to replace that entire line with: "LogLevel VERBOSE"
      4. If it does not, we will append a new line at the end: "LogLevel VERBOSE"
      5. Then use write_file to write the modified content back to /etc/ssh/sshd_config.

However, note that we cannot use the specific sed commands from the previous attempts. But we can use sed in a different way (as long as it's not the exact command string from the previous attempts).

We are allowed to use sed for a substitution that is not the exact string from the previous attempts.

   - To replace: we can use sed with a command that is not the exact string from the previous attempts.

But note: the previous attempts used specific sed commands. We are avoiding those exact strings, but we can use sed with a different command string.

Let's design:

  Step 1: Read the file -> use read_file to get the content of /etc/ssh/sshd_config.

  Step 2: Check for the LogLevel line -> use grep to look for the pattern: 
        pattern = r'^\s*LogLevel\s*='
        But note: we want to match the whole line? Actually, we can use grep to check if there's any line that matches.

        However, we can do: 
            if we find at least one line that matches, then we know we have to replace that line.
            But note: there might be multiple? We assume at most one.

        Alternatively, we can use grep to get the line number and then use sed to replace that specific line.

        We'll use: 
            grep -n '^\s*LogLevel\s*=' /etc/ssh/sshd_config   to get the line number.

        Then, if we get a line number, we can use sed to replace that line.

        The sed command we use: 
            sed -i '<line_number>s/.*/LogLevel VERBOSE/'   -> but wait, we want to replace the whole line.

        Actually, we can do: 
            sed -i '<line_number>s/.*/LogLevel VERBOSE/'   -> this will replace the entire line at line_number with "LogLevel VERBOSE"

        But note: the sed command string we are using is:

        This string is not the same as the ones used in the previous attempts.

        Previous attempts used:
            Attempt 1: sed -i "s/^s*#?s*LogLevels*.*$/LogLevel VERBOSE/"   -> this is a substitution command (s/old/new/)
            Attempt 2: sed -i '/^\s*LogLevel\s\+/Id'   -> this is also a substitution command (s/old/new/) but with a different pattern.

        Our sed command: 
            We are using: '<line_number>s/.*/LogLevel VERBOSE/'   -> this is also a substitution command, but the pattern is different: we are matching the entire line (.*/) and replacing with "LogLevel VERBOSE".

        So the sed command string is different.
